title: VibeProteinBench: An Evaluation Benchmark for Language-interfaced Vibe Protein Design
summary: Protein design aims to compose amino-acid sequences that fold into stable three-dimensional structures while satisfying targeted functional properties. The field is increasingly shifting toward vibe protein design, where a single model is expected to generate novel sequences, engineer existing proteins, and reason about protein characteristics through flexible natural-language constraints. Large language models (LLMs) have emerged as a leading paradigm in this space. However, existing evaluation benchmarks often limit their scope to a partial aspect of protein design, while others restrict design objectives to structured input schemas, lacking an integrated framework that evaluates the broad spectrum of protein design competence under open-ended intents. To this end, we present Vibe Protein design Benchmark (VibeProteinBench), a language-interfaced benchmark that probes generalist capabilities through three complementary stages mirroring a computational protein design workflow: recognition, engineering, and generation. Each stage is grounded in expert-curated mechanistic rationales and multi-faceted in silico validation, to computationally verify whether model outputs are biologically plausible. Evaluations across diverse general-purpose and domain-specialized LLMs reveal that no model achieves strong performance across all three stages, suggesting that generalist protein design remains a substantial open challenge for current LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VibeProteinBench, a comprehensive language-interfaced benchmark for evaluating large language models on protein design across three stages—recognition, engineering, and generation—revealing that no current model excels at all tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for protein design using LLMs are limited in scope, focusing on partial aspects or restricting design objectives to structured schemas. There is a need for an integrated framework that evaluates generalist protein design capabilities under open-ended, natural-language intents.
AI.method: The authors propose VibeProteinBench, a benchmark with three complementary stages (recognition, engineering, generation) that mirror a computational protein design workflow. Each stage is grounded in expert-curated mechanistic rationales and validated using multi-faceted in silico methods to assess biological plausibility.
AI.result: Evaluations of diverse general-purpose and domain-specialized LLMs show that no single model achieves strong performance across all three stages, indicating that generalist protein design remains a significant open challenge.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are not yet capable of generalist protein design, and VibeProteinBench provides a much-needed unified evaluation framework to drive progress in this direction.

title: Statin Recommendations among US Adults with the 2026 Dyslipidemia Guidelines
summary:
Importance: The 2026 multisociety dyslipidemia guideline recommended the PREVENT equations in place of the PCE equations, introduced 30-year risk assessment as a new treatment pathway, and lowered risk-based treatment thresholds. The net population impact of these concurrent changes on statin recommendations is unknown.
  Objective: To estimate changes in statin recommendations under 2026 PREVENT-based dyslipidemia guidelines compared with 2018 PCE-based guidelines.
  Design and Participants: Cross-sectional analysis of pooled data from NHANES, spanning 2011-2023 and comprising 24,199 participants aged 30-79 years.
  Main Outcomes and Measures: Number and proportion of US adults receiving or recommended for statin therapy.
  Results: At the class 1 threshold, the number of US adults receiving or recommended for statin therapy decreased by an estimated 3.0 million (95% CI, 2.3 million to 3.6 million), with larger reductions among Black adults (-4.2 percentage points [pp]), men (-4.0pp), and adults aged 50-69 years (-5.6pp). At the class 2 threshold--which additionally recommends statins for adults aged 30-59 years based on 30-year risk--the number of adults recommended increased by an estimated 20.8 million (95% CI, 19.6 million to 22.0 million), or +11.6pp. The increase was largest among adults aged 50-59 years (+19.7pp) and 40-49 years (+14.8pp).
  Conclusions: The net population impact of the 2026 dyslipidemia guidelines depends critically on which recommendation class is applied. At the class 1 threshold, statin recommendations decreased modestly; at the class 2 threshold, inclusion of 30-year risk assessment substantially expanded recommendations, particularly among younger adults. These divergent effects underscore the importance of the 30-year risk criterion as a major driver of new eligibility and the need for outcomes and equity monitoring during guideline implementation.

AI.tldr: The 2026 dyslipidemia guideline (using PREVENT equations) reduces statin recommendations by ~3 million adults under class 1 thresholds but increases recommendations by ~20.8 million under class 2 thresholds (which adds 30-year risk assessment for adults aged 30-59), with notable demographic disparities.
AI.motivation: To quantify the net population impact of concurrent changes in the 2026 multisociety dyslipidemia guidelines—switching from PCE to PREVENT equations, introducing 30-year risk assessment, and lowering treatment thresholds—on statin recommendations compared to the 2018 guidelines.
AI.method: Cross-sectional analysis of pooled NHANES data (2011-2023) with 24,199 participants aged 30-79 years, comparing statin recommendation rates under 2018 PCE-based vs. 2026 PREVENT-based guidelines.
AI.result: Class 1 threshold: ~3.0 million fewer adults recommended statins (larger reductions in Black adults [-4.2pp], men [-4.0pp], ages 50-69 [-5.6pp]). Class 2 threshold (including 30-year risk): ~20.8 million more adults recommended (+11.6pp), largest increases in ages 50-59 (+19.7pp) and 40-49 (+14.8pp).
AI.conclusion: The net impact depends critically on which recommendation class is applied; the 30-year risk criterion is the major driver of expanded eligibility, highlighting the need for outcomes and equity monitoring during implementation.

title: Internally triggered retrospective learning in neural networks
summary: Learning in artificial neural networks usually relies on continuous, externally driven weight updates, in which parameters are modified at every step in response to incoming data, error signals or reward feedback. In this setting, routine and informative inputs contribute similarly to parameter adjustment. We introduce a learning approach in which parameter updates are governed by internally generated events arising from the network own representational dynamics. During ongoing activity, synaptic interactions are accumulated as latent traces encoding recent coactivation patterns, without immediately modifying the underlying parameters. In parallel, an internal predictive process estimates the evolving latent state, while a scalar measure of discrepancy between predicted and observed states is continuously computed. When discrepancy exceeds an adaptive threshold derived from recent error statistics, a learning event is triggered, inducing a retrospective update selectively integrating past activity into the current configuration. We performed simulations using a minimal neural network exposed to structured sequential inputs with transient perturbations. We found that learning occurs through sparse, temporally localized events associated with increases in prediction error, leading to stepwise changes in synaptic efficacy and discrete transitions in latent state organization. By selectively reorganizing parameters in response to internally detected discrepancies, our episodic updating may reduce unnecessary parameter drift while preserving informative patterns. Potential applications include systems requiring selective adaptation to rare or informative inputs such as physiological, industrial or environmental monitoring, edge computing under limited energy budgets, autonomous systems operating in dynamic conditions and sequential computational data processing.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an episodic learning approach where artificial neural network parameters are updated only when internally detected prediction errors exceed an adaptive threshold, rather than at every step, resulting in sparse, event-driven weight updates triggered by representational discrepancies.
AI.motivation: Standard neural network learning relies on continuous, externally driven weight updates that treat routine and informative inputs similarly, causing unnecessary parameter drift and inefficient adaptation.
AI.method: The method accumulates synaptic interactions as latent traces without immediate parameter modification, computes internal prediction error against an evolving state estimate, and triggers a retrospective learning event only when the discrepancy exceeds an adaptive threshold derived from recent error statistics.
AI.result: Simulations on a minimal neural network with structured sequential inputs and perturbations showed learning occurs through sparse, temporally localized events correlated with prediction error spikes, leading to stepwise synaptic changes and discrete latent state transitions.
AI.conclusion: Episodic updating reduces unnecessary parameter drift while preserving informative patterns, with potential applications in selective adaptation scenarios such as physiological/environmental monitoring, edge computing, autonomous systems, and sequential data processing.

title: SCOPE: Siamese Contrastive Operon Pair Embeddings for Functional Sequence Representation and Classification
summary: Identifying operons is a fundamental step in understanding prokaryotic gene regulation, as classifying genes into operons supports the reconstruction of regulatory networks, functional annotation of unannotated genes, and drug candidate development. Experimental approaches such as RT-PCR and RNA-seq provide precise evidence of operon structure, but are laborious and largely limited to well-studied model organisms, making scalable computational methods essential for genome-wide operon identification. Prior computational approaches have employed traditional classifiers such as logistic regression and decision trees, motivating our use of these as physicochemical baselines. The DGEB benchmark evaluates operonic pair classification by embedding each sequence independently with a pre-trained protein language model and computing pairwise cosine similarity. In contrast, our Siamese MLP learns a classifier over the fused embedding space, which is theoretically better motivated for binary classification, as cosine similarity can yield meaningless scores depending on the regularization of the embedding model. While protein language model embeddings substantially outperform physicochemical features in ROC-AUC, a learned Siamese MLP head does not significantly improve over unsupervised cosine similarity in Average Precision, suggesting that the geometry of the embedding space already captures the functional relationships needed for this task. Nonetheless, our Siamese MLP achieves a ROC-AUC of 0.71, competitive with state-of-the-art models on the DGEB leaderboard. These findings indicate that protein language model embeddings are a viable, scalable foundation for operonic pair classification across diverse microbial genomes, with implications for automated genome annotation, regulatory network reconstruction, and characterization of organisms lacking experimental operon annotations.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates operon prediction using protein language model embeddings, showing they outperform physicochemical features, but a learned Siamese MLP classifier does not significantly beat unsupervised cosine similarity, achieving competitive ROC-AUC of 0.71 on the DGEB benchmark.
AI.motivation: Identifying operons is critical for understanding prokaryotic gene regulation, but experimental methods are labor-intensive and limited to well-studied organisms, necessitating scalable computational approaches.
AI.method: The authors use a Siamese MLP that learns a classifier over fused protein language model embeddings, compared against unsupervised cosine similarity on the DGEB benchmark, with physicochemical baselines from traditional classifiers.
AI.result: Protein language model embeddings substantially outperform physicochemical features in ROC-AUC, but the learned Siamese MLP does not significantly improve over unsupervised cosine similarity in Average Precision, achieving a ROC-AUC of 0.71.
AI.conclusion: Protein language model embeddings are a viable, scalable foundation for operonic pair classification across diverse microbial genomes, with implications for automated genome annotation and regulatory network reconstruction.

title: Morpho-Physiological and Genetic Diversity of Crataegus Taxa (Rosaceae) in Selected Locations of Iraqi Kurdistan-Region
summary: One of the great phytogeography zones of semi-arid lands in the world is the Kurdistan region of Iraq which hosts many important fruit species due to its geographical location and ecology. Mountain Hawthorn (Crataegus spp.) is a vital wild edible deciduous fruit tree of the genus Crataegus for the region, which is highly beneficial for ornamental, economical, industrial and medicinal uses. In the present study, morphological, phytochemical and molecular marker systems were applied on sixty-one Hawthorn accessions from different locations in the Iraqi Kurdistan region during April 2022 to September 2023. Phenotypic markers have proven to be extremely useful in studies of genetic diversity in Hawthorn genotypes, the results of the present morphological study showed that there are seven taxa (five species, two hybrids) were observed including, Crataegus azarolus, Crataegus meyrei, Crataegus monogyna, Crataegus orientalists, Crataegus pentagyna, Crataegus azarolus x Crataegus meyrei and Crataegus azarolus x Crataegus pentagyna. There was significant variation among different ecotypes in terms of plant type, reproductive stage, and fruit morphology and production uses. Fruit Physio-morphological data revealed a high level of significant variability (P 0.01) among accessions based on the analysis of variance. The most important characteristics for explaining fruit morphological variability `were 11 varbales including fruit weight (FW), fruit length (FL), fruit width (FW), seed length (SL), seed width (SW), number of seeds per fruits (NSF), volume solution (VS), fruit fresh weight (WOF), seed weight (WS), Potentional of hydrogen (pH) and mositure content (MC). They all are significantly different for all the traits measured for the studied accessions.

AI.tldr: This study analyzed 61 Hawthorn accessions from Iraqi Kurdistan using morphological, phytochemical, and molecular markers, identifying seven taxa (five species, two hybrids) with significant variation in fruit traits like weight, length, width, seed characteristics, pH, and moisture content.
AI.motivation: The Kurdistan region of Iraq is a major phytogeography zone with important fruit species, but Mountain Hawthorn (Crataegus spp.)—a valuable wild edible tree with ornamental, economic, industrial, and medicinal uses—lacks comprehensive genetic diversity assessment in this region.
AI.method: Morphological, phytochemical, and molecular marker systems were applied to 61 Hawthorn accessions from different locations in Iraqi Kurdistan from April 2022 to September 2023. Phenotypic markers and fruit physio-morphological data were analyzed.
AI.result: Seven taxa were identified (five species: Crataegus azarolus, C. meyrei, C. monogyna, C. orientalists, C. pentagyna; two hybrids: C. azarolus x C. meyrei and C. azarolus x C. pentagyna). Significant variation (P<0.01) was found across accessions for 11 key fruit traits including fruit weight, length, width, seed length/width, seed number, volume, fresh weight, seed weight, pH, and moisture content.
AI.conclusion: The studied Hawthorn accessions in Iraqi Kurdistan exhibit high genetic diversity with seven distinct taxa, and the identified morphological and physio-chemical traits (especially 11 key fruit variables) are effective for characterizing and distinguishing among these accessions.

title: The Same Problem by Different Names: Unifying Regression Dilution and Regression to the Mean
summary: Regression to the Mean and Regression Dilution are often viewed as unrelated issues in the clinical and ecological literatures. In reality, they are different names for the same problem: measurement error in an independent variable that biases the perceived relationship between two factors. This study unifies these traditions by comparing specialized clinical tools, like the Berry correction, with standard structural estimators such as Major Axis and Reduced Major Axis regression. Using an analytical framework, we evaluate how these methods perform across various noise levels and sample sizes. Our results show that the Berry method is a specialized tool designed for clinical scenarios where a 1:1 relationship is expected. However, applying it to ecological trade-offs with negative slopes can lead to severe errors. We provide maps of optimality to identify which estimator most accurately recovers the true biological signal under different conditions. By reconciling these disparate methods, we offer a principled guide for researchers to choose the correct tool based on their data's noise profile rather than their disciplinary tradition.

AI.tldr: This paper unifies Regression to the Mean (clinical) and Regression Dilution (ecological) as the same measurement error problem, comparing the Berry correction with standard structural estimators (MA, RMA). The Berry method works for 1:1 relationships but fails for negative-slope ecological trade-offs. Optimality maps guide tool selection by noise profile rather than discipline.
AI.motivation: Clinical and ecological literatures treat Regression to the Mean and Regression Dilution as unrelated problems, despite both stemming from measurement error in independent variables. This disconnect leads researchers to apply inappropriate tools based on disciplinary tradition rather than statistical appropriateness.
AI.method: The study analytically compares the Berry correction (clinical tool) with Major Axis (MA) and Reduced Major Axis (RMA) regression (standard structural estimators) across varying noise levels and sample sizes. Optimality maps are generated to identify the best estimator for recovering true biological signals under different conditions.
AI.result: The Berry method is optimal only for clinical scenarios expecting a 1:1 relationship, but applying it to ecological trade-offs with negative slopes produces severe errors. MA and RMA perform better in different regions of the noise parameter space.
AI.conclusion: Researchers should choose estimation tools based on their data's noise profile, not disciplinary tradition. The study provides principled guidance (optimality maps) for selecting the correct method.

title: Beyond Manual Curation: Augmenting Targeted Protein Degradation Databases via Agentic Literature Extraction Workflows
summary: Predictive models in biomedicine depend on structured assay data locked in the text, tables, and supplements of primary publications. This bottleneck is especially acute in targeted protein degradation (TPD), where each assay record must combine compound identity, degradation target, recruiter, assay context, and endpoint values reported across sections, tables, and supplementary files. Inconsistent compound identifiers and incomplete or implicit assay context further demand domain-specific logic that generic LLM pipelines do not provide. Existing molecular glue and PROTAC databases are manually curated and often lack the experimental context required for downstream modeling. We formulate TPD database extraction as a domain-specific curation task and present an expert-in-the-loop LLM workflow, evaluated through a triangular comparison among LLM predictions, standardized baseline records, and expert-annotated ground truth. A lightweight cross-validated prompt-refinement module adapts extraction instructions from scarce expert annotations. With only seven annotated molecular glue publications, the workflow achieved record-level $F_1 = 0.98$ and transferred to PROTACs by terminology substitution alone, maintaining record-level $F_1 > 0.93$. Applied at scale, it expanded molecular glue and PROTAC databases by 81% and 92% records, respectively, with 92% and 82.5% of newly recovered records validated as correct upon expert review. The workflow also recovered kinetic and assay-context information essential for cross-study potency comparison and condition-aware degradation modeling. We release the workflow, prompts, evaluation code, and extracted datasets as resources for TPD data curation and AI-assisted scientific curation more broadly.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an expert-in-the-loop LLM workflow for extracting structured assay data from targeted protein degradation (TPD) publications, achieving high accuracy (F1=0.98) with minimal expert annotations and expanding existing databases by 81-92%.
AI.motivation: Structured assay data critical for predictive biomedicine is locked in text/tables/supplements of publications; this bottleneck is acute in TPD where records require compound identity, target, recruiter, assay context, and endpoint values with inconsistent identifiers and implicit context that generic LLMs cannot handle.
AI.method: An expert-in-the-loop LLM workflow with a cross-validated prompt-refinement module that adapts extraction instructions from scarce expert annotations; evaluated via triangular comparison among LLM predictions, baseline records, and expert-annotated ground truth.
AI.result: With only 7 annotated molecular glue publications, achieved record-level F1=0.98; transferred to PROTACs by terminology substitution alone (F1>0.93). Expanded molecular glue and PROTAC databases by 81% and 92% records respectively, with 92% and 82.5% validated as correct upon expert review.
AI.conclusion: The workflow effectively extracts kinetic and assay-context information essential for cross-study potency comparison and condition-aware degradation modeling; the authors release the workflow, prompts, evaluation code, and extracted datasets as resources for AI-assisted scientific curation.

title: Scalable vertex guided filtrations identify structurally relevant genes in cancer networks
summary: Topological data analysis (TDA) has established itself as a useful tool for capturing multiscale structures in complex networks, such as connected components, cycles, and cavities. Although Vietoris-Rips (VR) filtering is widely used in network analysis, it tends to be computationally expensive, especially for large networks. This work explores vertex function-based (VFB) filtering based on network measures, applying persistent homology to identify relevant topological structures in cancer-associated protein networks, and compares its effectiveness with the VR approach. The results show that VFB reproduces the second-order structures (Betti-2) identified by VR, recovering previously reported essential genes. In addition, VFB detected new driver genes, confirmed in databases such as IntOGen and NCG, and allowed analysis of third-order structures (Betti-3) that was not feasible with VR. Thus, VFB represents a scalable alternative to VR, preserving biological interpretability and complementing classical network metrics.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a vertex function-based (VFB) filtering method for persistent homology in topological data analysis, showing it is computationally cheaper than Vietoris-Rips (VR) filtering and can detect higher-order topological structures (Betti-3) and new driver genes in cancer protein networks that VR cannot handle.
AI.motivation: Vietoris-Rips (VR) filtering is computationally expensive for large networks, limiting its scalability. The authors aim to develop a more efficient alternative for topological analysis of complex biological networks.
AI.method: Vertex function-based (VFB) filtering uses network measures (vertex functions) to build filtration, then applies persistent homology to extract topological features. The method is compared against standard VR filtering on cancer-associated protein networks.
AI.result: VFB reproduces second-order structures (Betti-2) found by VR and recovers known essential genes. Additionally, VFB detects new driver genes validated in IntOGen/NCG databases and enables analysis of third-order structures (Betti-3) that VR could not compute.
AI.conclusion: VFB filtering is a scalable, biologically interpretable alternative to VR filtering for TDA on large networks, complementing classical network metrics.

title: NORI: Fast probabilistic inference for ambiguous observation-entity mappings
summary: NORI performs probabilistic inference to resolve ambiguous mappings between experimental observations and biological entities orders of magnitude faster than state-of-the-art methods. This makes large-scale analysis and extensive hyperparameter optimization possible, and supports a broader range of bioinformatics applications, including protein inference, taxonomic and functional analysis in omics-fields.

AI.tldr: NORI is a probabilistic inference method that resolves ambiguous biological mappings much faster than existing methods, enabling large-scale analysis and broader bioinformatics applications.
AI.motivation: To resolve ambiguous mappings between experimental observations and biological entities efficiently, as existing methods are too slow for large-scale analysis and hyperparameter optimization.
AI.method: Probabilistic inference approach for resolving ambiguous mappings between experimental observations and biological entities.
AI.result: Orders of magnitude faster than state-of-the-art methods, making large-scale analysis and extensive hyperparameter optimization possible.
AI.conclusion: Supports a broader range of bioinformatics applications, including protein inference, taxonomic and functional analysis in omics-fields.

title: Accounting for Missed Events in the Bayesian Modeling of IP3R Multimodal Gating
summary: The Inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate receptor channel (IP 3 R) is an important calcium channel involved in calcium-induced calcium release, playing a prominent role in intracellular calcium signaling. However, accurately characterizing its gating behavior remains a challenge, particularly due to the temporal resolution of patch clamp techniques that is not large enough to detect all short-lived events. This limitation can significantly bias the inference of kinetic models describing the receptor activity. To address this issue, we focused on the quantitative analysis of IP 3 R gating behavior using patch clamp data, with particular attention to missed events. We modeled IP 3 R channel gating using Hierarchical Markov chains and used a Bayesian approach that integrates missed event correction directly into the likelihood function, enabling more accurate parameter inference and model evaluation. We show that accounting for missed events deeply clarifies the multi-modal model that emerges from model selection. In this new model, the Park and Drive modes both consist of the same 3-state Markov model, with mode-dependent kinetic parameters: the Drive mode stabilizes the closed state directly connected to the open one, whereas the Park mode stabilizes the other closed state, that is not connected to the open one. Intermediate Ca 2+ concentrations are found to strongly depress the Drive to Park transition rate, so that the IP 3 R channel undergoes frequent transitions to the Park mode only for __ 50 nM or micromolar Ca 2+ concentrations. Overall, our approach provides a refined perspective on IP 3 R channel modeling and highlights the critical importance of accounting for missed events upon model selection based on single-channel recordings.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a Bayesian method with missed event correction to model IP3R channel gating using Hierarchical Markov chains, revealing a refined multi-modal model where Park and Drive modes share a 3-state structure but differ in which closed state is stabilized.
AI.motivation: Accurately characterizing IP3R gating behavior is challenging due to temporal resolution limits of patch clamp techniques that miss short-lived events, significantly biasing kinetic model inference.
AI.method: Modeled IP3R channel gating using Hierarchical Markov chains with a Bayesian approach that integrates missed event correction directly into the likelihood function.
AI.result: Accounting for missed events clarifies the multi-modal model: both Park and Drive modes share a 3-state Markov structure but with mode-dependent kinetic parameters. Intermediate Ca2+ concentrations depress Drive-to-Park transition rates.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a refined perspective on IP3R channel modeling and highlights the critical importance of accounting for missed events in model selection based on single-channel recordings.

title: Self-organized MT Direction Maps Emerge from Spatiotemporal Contrastive Optimization
summary: The spatial and functional organization of the primate visual cortex is a fundamental problem in neuroscience. While recent computational frameworks like the Topographic Deep Artificial Neural Network (TDANN) have successfully modeled spatial organization in the ventral stream, the computational origins of the dorsal stream's distinct topographies, such as direction-selective maps in the middle temporal (MT) area, remain largely unresolved. In this work, we present a spatiotemporal TDANN to investigate whether MT topography is governed by the same universal principles. By training a 3D ResNet on naturalistic videos via a Momentum Contrast (MoCo) self-supervised paradigm alongside a biologically inspired spatial loss, we demonstrate the spontaneous emergence of brain-like direction maps and topological pinwheel structures. Crucially, we reveal that MT tuning properties, characterized by strong direction selectivity paired with a residual axial component, arise from a strict optimization trade-off between task-driven discriminative pressure and spatial regularization. The model's representations quantitatively match in vivo macaque MT physiological baselines, including direction selectivity index, circular variance, and pinwheel density. These findings unify the computational origins of the ventral and dorsal streams, establishing a general mechanism for cortical self-organization.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a spatiotemporal Topographic Deep Artificial Neural Network (TDANN) that models the emergence of direction-selective maps and pinwheel structures in the primate MT area, showing that dorsal stream topography arises from the same trade-off between task-driven discriminative learning and spatial regularization that governs ventral stream organization.
AI.motivation: While the ventral stream's topographic organization has been successfully modeled by TDANN frameworks, the computational origins of the dorsal stream's distinct topographies (e.g., direction-selective maps in MT) remain largely unresolved.
AI.method: The authors train a 3D ResNet on naturalistic videos using a Momentum Contrast (MoCo) self-supervised paradigm combined with a biologically inspired spatial loss to create a spatiotemporal TDANN.
AI.result: The model spontaneously produces brain-like direction maps and topological pinwheel structures. MT tuning properties (strong direction selectivity with residual axial component) arise from an optimization trade-off between task-driven pressure and spatial regularization. Representations quantitatively match macaque MT physiological baselines.
AI.conclusion: The findings unify the computational origins of ventral and dorsal streams, establishing a general mechanism for cortical self-organization.

title: Set-Aggregated Genome Embeddings for Microbiome Abundance Prediction
summary: Microbiome functions are encoded within the genes of the community-wide metagenome. A natural question is whether properties of a microbial community can be predicted just from knowing the raw DNA sequences of its members. In this work, we employ set-aggregated genome embeddings (SAGE) to predict community-level abundance profiles, exploiting the few-shot learning capabilities of genomic language models (GLMs). We benchmark this approach to show improved generalization on novel genomes compared to classical bioinformatics approaches. Model ablation shows that community-level latent representations directly result in improved performance. Lastly, we demonstrate the benefits of intermediate transformations between latent representations and demonstrate the differences between GLM embedding choices.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SAGE, using genome embeddings from genomic language models with few-shot learning to predict microbial community abundance profiles, showing improved generalization over classical methods.
AI.motivation: To determine if raw DNA sequences alone can predict microbial community properties, moving beyond classical bioinformatics approaches that require reference databases and annotations.
AI.method: Set-aggregated genome embeddings (SAGE) leveraging genomic language models (GLMs) with few-shot learning to predict community-level abundance profiles from raw DNA sequences.
AI.result: Improved generalization on novel genomes compared to classical bioinformatics approaches; community-level latent representations directly improve performance; intermediate transformations between latent representations are beneficial.
AI.conclusion: Genomic language models with set aggregation can effectively predict community properties from raw sequences, with advantages for novel genomes and interpretable latent representations.

title: Empirical scaling laws in balanced networks with conductance-based synapses
summary: Strongly coupled, recurrent, balanced network models have been successful in describing and predicting many phenomena observed in cortical neural recordings. However, most balanced network models use current-based synapse models in place of more realistic, conductance-based models. Conductance-based synapse models predict unrealistically small membrane potential variability. On the other hand, introducing realistic levels of spike time correlations to models with current-based synapses predicts unrealistically large membrane potential variability. We use computer simulations to show that these two effects can cancel: Recurrent network models with conductance-based synapses and spike time correlations produce more realistic, moderate levels of membrane potential variability. Consistent with recent work on feedforward networks, our results show that including more realistic modeling assumptions produces more realistic dynamics, but only if when two modeling assumptions are included together.

AI.tldr: Balanced network models with conductance-based synapses and spike time correlations cancel out unrealistic membrane potential variability, producing more realistic dynamics than models with only one of these features.
AI.motivation: Current-based balanced network models produce unrealistic membrane potential variability when spike time correlations are introduced, while conductance-based models underestimate variability. The paper seeks to reconcile these issues by combining both modeling approaches.
AI.method: Computer simulations comparing recurrent network models with conductance-based synapses and spike time correlations against models with only current-based synapses or only conductance-based synapses.
AI.result: Combining conductance-based synapses with spike time correlations cancels out their individual unrealistic effects, producing moderate, realistic levels of membrane potential variability.
AI.conclusion: Including multiple realistic modeling assumptions together produces more realistic dynamics than including them individually, highlighting the importance of combined realistic features in neural network models.

title: Letting the neural code speak: Automated characterization of monkey visual neurons through human language
summary: Understanding what individual neurons encode is a core question in neuroscience. In primary visual cortex (V1), mathematical models (e.g., Gabor functions) capture neural selectivity, but no comparable framework exists for higher areas. We show that natural language can fill this role: across macaque V1 and V4, the selectivity of most neurons is captured by concise, verifiable semantic descriptions. Using digital twins of V1 and V4, we develop a closed-loop framework that translates each neuron's high- and low-activating images into dense captions, generates a semantic hypothesis and synthesized images, and verifies the hypothesis in silico. Descriptions range from oriented edges and spatial frequency in V1 to conjunctions of form, color, and texture in V4. In V4, images generated from activating and suppressing hypotheses drove 96.1% of neurons above the 95th and 97.6% below the 5th percentile of natural-image responses, respectively (vs. ~10\% for random images); V1 activation results matched V4, while V1 suppression was less describable in language. Representational similarity analysis reveals partial alignment between neural activity, vision embeddings, and language embeddings, with vision most aligned to neural activity; alignment lost in the text bottleneck is recovered when hypotheses are rendered back into images, showing that linguistic compression is lossy yet semantically faithful. Together, these results show that combining generative models with neural digital twins enables interpretable, testable descriptions of neural function at scale, toward agentic scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that natural language can effectively capture the selectivity of neurons in visual cortex areas V1 and V4 by using digital twins and generative models to create verifiable semantic descriptions, with high success rates in driving neural responses.
AI.motivation: Understanding what individual neurons encode is a core question in neuroscience. While mathematical models (e.g., Gabor functions) capture neural selectivity in primary visual cortex (V1), no comparable framework exists for higher visual areas.
AI.method: Using digital twins of macaque V1 and V4, the authors develop a closed-loop framework that: (1) translates each neuron's high/low-activating images into dense captions, (2) generates a semantic hypothesis and synthesized images, and (3) verifies the hypothesis in silico.
AI.result: In V4, images generated from activating/suppressing hypotheses drove 96.1% of neurons above the 95th percentile and 97.6% below the 5th percentile of natural-image responses. V1 activation results matched V4, but suppression was less describable. Representational similarity analysis shows vision embeddings align most with neural activity, and linguistic compression is lossy yet semantically faithful.
AI.conclusion: Combining generative models with neural digital twins enables interpretable, testable descriptions of neural function at scale, toward agentic scientific discovery.

title: Interpretable EEG Microstate Discovery via Variational Deep Embedding: A Systematic Architecture Search with Multi-Quadrant Evaluation
summary: EEG microstate analysis segments continuous brain electrical activity into brief, quasi-stable topographic configurations that reflect discrete functional brain states. Conventional approaches such as Modified K-Means operate directly in electrode space with hard assignment, offering no learned latent representation, no generative decoder, and no mechanism to decode latent configurations into verifiable scalp topographies, limiting both model transparency and interpretability. To address this, we present a Convolutional Variational Deep Embedding (Conv-VaDE) model that jointly learns topographic reconstruction and probabilistic soft clustering in a shared latent space. Conv-VaDE enables generative decoding of cluster prototypes into verifiable scalp topographies, replacing opaque hard partitioning with probabilistic soft assignment. A polarity invariance scheme and a four-dimensional grid search over cluster count (K from 3 to 20), latent dimensionality, network depth, and channel width are conducted to systematically reveal how each architectural design choice shapes the quality, stability, and interpretability of learned EEG microstate representations. The model is evaluated on the LEMON resting-state eyes-closed EEG dataset with ten participants using topographic template formation, clustering stability, and global explained variance (GEV). The architecture search reveals that depth L = 4 appears consistently across all 18 best-performing configurations, yielding a best-case GEV of 0.730 and a silhouette of 0.229 at K = 4 across the model sweeps, where moderately deep networks with compact channel widths and small latent dimensionality dominate across the full K range. These results establish that principled architecture search, rather than model scale, is the key to interpretable and stable EEG microstate discovery via variational deep embedding.

AI.tldr: Conv-VaDE is a variational deep embedding model for EEG microstate analysis that jointly learns topographic reconstruction and probabilistic soft clustering. Architecture search reveals depth L=4 is optimal, achieving best GEV of 0.730 and silhouette of 0.229 at K=4 on the LEMON dataset.
AI.motivation: Conventional EEG microstate analysis methods (e.g., Modified K-Means) operate in electrode space with hard assignment, offering no learned latent representation, no generative decoder, and no mechanism to verify scalp topographies, limiting transparency and interpretability.
AI.method: Conv-VaDE integrates convolutional variational autoencoding with deep embedding clustering, learning a shared latent space for both topographic reconstruction and probabilistic soft clustering. A polarity invariance scheme and 4D grid search (K: 3-20, latent dim, depth, channel width) were used to systematically explore architectural impacts.
AI.result: Depth L=4 appears in all 18 best-performing configurations. Best-case GEV=0.730, silhouette=0.229 at K=4 across sweeps. Moderate depth, compact channel widths, and small latent dimensionality dominate across the full K range.
AI.conclusion: Principled architecture search, rather than model scale, is key to interpretable and stable EEG microstate discovery via variational deep embedding.

title: Structural Interpretations of Protein Language Model Representations via Differentiable Graph Partitioning
comment: 19 Pages, 8 figures, 11 Tables, Submitted to NeurIPS 2026
summary: Protein language models such as ESM-2 learn rich residue representations that achieve strong performance on protein function prediction, but their features remain difficult to interpret as structural $\&$ evolutionary signals are encoded in dense latent spaces. We propose a plug-$\&$-play framework that projects ESM-2 representations onto protein contact graphs $\&$ applies $\textbf{SoftBlobGIN}$, a lightweight Graph Isomorphism Network with differentiable Gumbel-softmax substructure pooling, to perform structure-aware message passing $\&$ learn coarse functional substructures for downstream prediction tasks. Across enzyme classification, SoftBlobGIN achieves 92.8\% accuracy $\&$ 0.898 macro-F1. Unlike post hoc analysis of protein language models alone, our method produces directly auditable structural explanations: GNNExplainer recovers biologically meaningful active-site residues, spatially localized functional clusters, $\&$ catalytic contact patterns. On binding-site detection, SoftBlobGIN improves residue AUROC from $0.885$ using an ESM-2 linear probe to $0.983$, indicating that these structural explanations are not recoverable from language-model features alone. Learned blob partitions provide an additional layer of interpretability by automatically grouping residues into functional substructures, with blobs containing annotated active-site residues showing $1.85\times$ higher importance than other blobs ($ρ{=}0.339$, $p{=}0.009$), without any active-site supervision. Our framework requires no retraining of the language model, adds only $\sim$1.1M parameters, $\&$ generalises across ProteinShake tasks, achieving $F_{\max}$ of $0.733$ on Gene Ontology prediction $\&$ AUROC of $0.969$ on binding-site detection. We position this as an interpretable structural companion to protein language models that makes their predictions more transparent $\&$ auditable.

AI.tldr: SoftBlobGIN projects ESM-2 representations onto protein contact graphs with differentiable substructure pooling, achieving strong function prediction (92.8% enzyme accuracy, 0.983 binding-site AUROC) while providing interpretable structural explanations without retraining the language model.
AI.motivation: Protein language models like ESM-2 achieve strong performance but their dense latent representations are difficult to interpret for structural and evolutionary signals.
AI.method: A plug-and-play framework that projects ESM-2 features onto protein contact graphs and applies SoftBlobGIN, a Graph Isomorphism Network with Gumbel-softmax substructure pooling, for structure-aware message passing and learning functional substructures.
AI.result: 92.8% accuracy & 0.898 macro-F1 on enzyme classification; binding-site detection AUROC improves from 0.885 (ESM-2 linear probe) to 0.983; blob partitions show 1.85× higher importance for active-site residues without supervision; ~1.1M added parameters.
AI.conclusion: SoftBlobGIN serves as an interpretable structural companion to protein language models, making predictions more transparent and auditable through directly explainable structural features.

title: Deep Learning for Protein Complex Prediction and Design
summary: Accurately modeling and designing protein complex structures is a central problem in computational structural biology, with broad implications for understanding cellular function and developing therapeutics. This thesis investigates two fundamental aspects of this problem using deep learning: domain-specific architectures that capture the hierarchical nature of protein structures, and search algorithms that efficiently navigate the vast sequence spaces of protein complexes to identify interacting homologs for improving complex structure prediction and to design protein sequences.

AI.tldr: This thesis uses deep learning to model and design protein complex structures by developing domain-specific architectures and efficient search algorithms for identifying interacting homologs and designing protein sequences.
AI.motivation: Accurately modeling and designing protein complex structures is critical for understanding cellular function and developing therapeutics, representing a central challenge in computational structural biology.
AI.method: The thesis employs deep learning with two key approaches: (1) domain-specific architectures that capture the hierarchical nature of protein structures, and (2) search algorithms for navigating sequence spaces of protein complexes to identify interacting homologs and design sequences.
AI.result: Improved complex structure prediction through identification of interacting homologs and design of protein sequences via efficient navigation of sequence spaces.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning-based domain-specific architectures and search algorithms can effectively address fundamental problems in protein complex modeling and design, with implications for biology and therapeutics.

title: Unlocking LLM Creativity in Science through Analogical Reasoning
summary: Autonomous science promises to augment scientific discovery, particularly in complex fields like biomedicine. However, this requires AI systems that can consistently generate novel and diverse solutions to open-ended problems. We evaluate LLMs on the task of open-ended solution generation and quantify their tendency to mode collapse into low-diversity generations. To mitigate this mode collapse, we introduce analogical reasoning (AR) as a new approach to solution generation. AR generates analogies to cross-domain problems based on shared relational structure, then uses those analogies to search for novel solutions. Compared to baselines, AR discovers significantly more diverse generations (improving solution diversity metrics by 90-173%), generates novel solutions over 50% of the time (compared to as little as 1.6% for baselines), and produces high-quality analogies. To validate the real-world feasibility of AR, we implement AR-generated solutions across four biomedical problems, yielding consistent quantitative gains. AR-generated approaches achieve a nearly 13-fold improvement on distributional metrics for perturbation effect prediction, outperform all baselines on AUPRC when predicting cell-cell communication, infer brain region interactions with a high Spearman correlation ($ρ$=0.729) to published methods, and establish state-of-the-art performance on 2 datasets for oligonucleotide property prediction. The novel and diverse solutions produced by AR can be used to augment the search space of existing solution generation methods.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Analogical Reasoning (AR) to mitigate mode collapse in LLM-based open-ended solution generation for autonomous science. AR improves solution diversity by 90-173%, generates novel solutions >50% of the time, and achieves consistent quantitative gains across four biomedical problems including a 13-fold improvement in perturbation effect prediction.
AI.motivation: Autonomous science requires AI systems that can generate novel and diverse solutions to open-ended problems, but current LLMs tend to mode collapse into low-diversity generations, limiting their effectiveness for scientific discovery.
AI.method: Analogical Reasoning (AR) generates analogies to cross-domain problems based on shared relational structure, then uses those analogies to search for novel solutions, thereby expanding the diversity of generated solutions.
AI.result: AR improves solution diversity metrics by 90-173%, generates novel solutions over 50% of the time (vs. as little as 1.6% for baselines), achieves a nearly 13-fold improvement on perturbation effect prediction, outperforms baselines on AUPRC for cell-cell communication prediction, infers brain region interactions with Spearman ρ=0.729, and establishes SOTA on 2 datasets for oligonucleotide property prediction.
AI.conclusion: AR-generated novel and diverse solutions can effectively augment the search space of existing solution generation methods, demonstrating real-world feasibility across multiple biomedical problems.

title: LPDP: Inference-Time Reward Control for Variable-Length DNA Generation with Edit Flows
summary: We study the application of recent Edit Flows for inference-time reward control for DNA sequence generation. Unlike most reward-guided DNA generation frameworks, which operate on fixed-length sequence spaces, Edit Flows have a potential to generate variable-length DNA through biologically plausible insertion, deletion, and substitution operations. In particular, we propose Local Perturbation Discrete Programming (LPDP), a training-free, intermediate-state and action-aware local re-solving operator for variable-length DNA edit-action generators at inference time. More specifically, at each guided rollout step, LPDP scores one-step root edits, retains a near-best root band, and re-ranks each retained root by solving a bounded local discrete program around its child sequence. This local program uses the typed geometry of edit actions to focus on coherent substitution, insertion, or deletion subgraphs, and aggregates local continuations with either a hard Max backup or a soft log-sum-exponential (LSE) backup. We instantiate LPDP in two regimes: front-loaded reward tilting for enhancer optimization, where early edits are critical for establishing global regulatory sequence structure, and back-loaded reward tilting for exon-intron-exon inpainting, where late edits fine-tune splice-boundary contexts.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes LPDP, a training-free method for reward-guided DNA sequence generation using Edit Flows, enabling variable-length DNA generation with insertion/deletion/substitution operations via local discrete programming at inference time.
AI.motivation: Existing reward-guided DNA generation frameworks mostly operate on fixed-length sequence spaces, lacking the ability to generate biologically realistic variable-length DNA through natural editing operations like insertions, deletions, and substitutions.
AI.method: LPDP (Local Perturbation Discrete Programming) is a training-free inference-time operator that scores one-step root edits, retains a near-best root band, and re-ranks roots by solving bounded local discrete programs using typed edit geometry, with either Max or log-sum-exponential backups.
AI.result: The method is instantiated in two regimes: front-loaded reward tilting for enhancer optimization (early edits critical for global structure) and back-loaded reward tilting for exon-intron-exon inpainting (late edits fine-tune splice-boundary contexts).
AI.conclusion: LPDP enables effective inference-time reward control for variable-length DNA generation by leveraging Edit Flows and local discrete programming, addressing limitations of fixed-length frameworks.

title: Bistability, Absolute Concentration Robustness, and Hysteresis in Dual-Site Futile Cycles with Bifunctional Enzymes
summary: Bifunctional enzymes, which catalyze both the forward and reverse steps of a substrate modification reaction, arise naturally in bacterial two-component signaling systems and metabolic regulation. Beyond their well-known role in conferring absolute concentration robustness (ACR) on substrate species, bifunctional enzymes profoundly shape the dynamical landscape of the networks in which they appear. We study a class of dual-site futile cycles in which the reverse modification steps are carried out by bifunctional enzyme-substrate compounds, and provide a complete mathematical analysis of all four such networks, characterizing the existence, number, and stability of steady states, as well as the bifurcation structure as total substrate is varied. All four networks admit boundary steady states, in contrast to the non-bifunctional case. The networks differ in the number and stability of boundary steady states, in the maximum number of positive steady states (ranging from two to four), and in whether bistability is present. In two networks, a transcritical bifurcation connects the boundary and positive steady state branches; in one case this is a backward bifurcation, producing hysteresis. Perhaps the most striking phenomenon occurs in one of the four networks, which simultaneously exhibits bistability and ACR in the final modification state, where the system can settle into either of two stable steady states with different intermediate concentrations yet identical final product concentration.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a complete mathematical analysis of four dual-site futile cycle networks with bifunctional enzymes, revealing diverse steady-state behaviors including boundary states, bistability, transcritical bifurcations, and a novel phenomenon where bistability coexists with absolute concentration robustness (ACR).
AI.motivation: Bifunctional enzymes (catalyzing both forward and reverse steps) are known to confer ACR, but their broader dynamical effects on network behavior were not fully understood.
AI.method: Complete mathematical analysis of four dual-site futile cycle networks where reverse modification steps are carried out by bifunctional enzyme-substrate compounds.
AI.result: All four networks have boundary steady states (unlike non-bifunctional cases); they differ in number/stability of boundary states, maximum positive steady states (2-4), and presence of bistability; one network exhibits both bistability and ACR simultaneously.
AI.conclusion: Bifunctional enzymes create richer dynamical behavior than previously appreciated, including backward transcritical bifurcations producing hysteresis, and the surprising co-occurrence of bistability with ACR.

title: EpiCastBench: Datasets and Benchmarks for Multivariate Epidemic Forecasting
summary: The increasing adoption of data-driven decision-making in public health has established epidemic forecasting as a critical area of research. Recent advances in multivariate forecasting models better capture complex temporal dependencies than conventional univariate approaches, which model individual series independently. Despite this potential, the development of robust epidemic forecasting methods is constrained by the lack of high-quality benchmarks comprising diverse multivariate datasets across infectious diseases and geographical regions. To address this gap, we present EpiCastBench, a large-scale benchmarking framework featuring 40 curated (correlated) multivariate epidemic datasets. These publicly available datasets span a wide range of infectious diseases and exhibit diverse characteristics in terms of temporal granularity, series length, and sparsity. We analyze these datasets to identify their global features and structural patterns. To ensure reproducibility and fair comparison, we establish standardized evaluation settings, including a unified forecasting horizon, consistent preprocessing pipelines, diverse performance metrics, and statistical significance testing. By leveraging this framework, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of 15 multivariate forecasting models spanning statistical baselines to state-of-the-art deep learning and foundation models. All datasets and code are publicly available on Kaggle (https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/aimltsf/epicastbench) and GitHub (https://github.com/aimltsf/EpiCastBench).

AI.tldr: EpiCastBench is a large-scale benchmarking framework with 40 curated multivariate epidemic datasets for standardized evaluation of 15 forecasting models, from statistical to deep learning approaches.
AI.motivation: The lack of high-quality benchmarks comprising diverse multivariate epidemic datasets hinders the development of robust epidemic forecasting methods.
AI.method: Built 40 curated multivariate epidemic datasets with standardized evaluation settings (unified horizon, consistent preprocessing, diverse metrics, statistical testing); evaluated 15 models from statistical baselines to deep learning and foundation models.
AI.result: A comprehensive evaluation of 15 multivariate forecasting models across diverse epidemic datasets, with all resources publicly available on Kaggle and GitHub.
AI.conclusion: EpiCastBench provides reproducible, standardized benchmarks to advance multivariate epidemic forecasting research.

title: Decomposing the Generalization Gap in PROTAC Activity Prediction: Variance Attribution and the Inter-Laboratory Ceiling
comment: 32 pages, 11 figures, 11 tables. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/ThorKl/protac-bench (CC-BY-4.0). Code: https://github.com/ThorKlm/PROTAC-Bench (MIT)
summary: Machine-learning predictors of biochemical activity often exhibit large random-split-to-leave-one-target-out generalisation gaps that have been documented but not decomposed. We frame this as an evaluation-science question and use targeted protein degradation as the empirical test bed. PROTACs (proteolysis-targeting chimeras) are heterobifunctional small molecules that induce targeted protein degradation, with more than forty candidates currently in clinical trials; published predictors report AUROC of 0.85 to 0.91 under random-split cross-validation, while the leave-one-target-out (LOTO) protocol of Ribes et al. reduces performance to approximately 0.67. Random splits reward within-target interpolation, whereas LOTO measures the novel-target prediction that de-novo design depends on. We decompose this gap and identify inter-laboratory measurement variance as the dominant component, anchored by a within-target cross-laboratory cascade bounding the inter-laboratory contribution at 0.124 AUROC, well above the 0.05 contribution from binarisation-threshold choice. Across eight published architectures and ESM-2 protein language models up to 3B parameters, LOTO AUROC plateaus near 0.67, with a comparable plateau under SMILES-level deduplication; a 21-dimensional 2000-trial hyperparameter optimisation cannot break this ceiling, and the rank-1 single-seed configuration regresses by 0.161 AUROC under multi-seed validation, matching a closed-form selection-bias prediction (Bailey and Lopez de Prado, 2014). Few-shot k=5 stratified per-target retraining combined with ADMET features lifts 65-target LOTO AUROC from 0.668 to 0.7050, and post-hoc Platt scaling recovers raw output to within the 0.05 well-calibrated threshold. We release PROTAC-Bench (10,748 measurements, 173 targets, 65 LOTO folds), the variance-decomposition framework, the per-target calibration protocol, and the evaluation code.

AI.tldr: This paper decomposes the generalization gap between random-split and leave-one-target-out (LOTO) validation in PROTAC bioactivity prediction, finding inter-laboratory measurement variance is the dominant factor. Despite extensive optimization, LOTO AUROC plateaus near 0.67, with a small improvement to 0.705 using few-shot retraining and ADMET features.
AI.motivation: To decompose and explain the large performance gap between random-split and leave-one-target-out cross-validation in machine-learning predictors of biochemical activity, using PROTACs as a testbed. This gap has been documented but not systematically decomposed, and understanding it is critical for de-novo drug design that requires novel-target prediction.
AI.method: The authors frame the question as evaluation science, decompose the generalization gap using variance decomposition, quantify inter-laboratory measurement variance via a within-target cross-laboratory cascade, test 8 architectures and ESM-2 models up to 3B parameters, perform 21-dimensional 2000-trial hyperparameter optimization, and develop a few-shot k=5 stratified per-target retraining protocol with ADMET features and Platt scaling.
AI.result: Inter-laboratory measurement variance is the dominant component (bounding inter-laboratory contribution at 0.124 AUROC), far exceeding binarization-threshold choice (0.05). LOTO AUROC plateaus near 0.67 across all architectures, and hyperparameter optimization cannot break this ceiling. Few-shot retraining with ADMET features lifts LOTO AUROC from 0.668 to 0.7050, and Platt scaling recovers calibration.
AI.conclusion: The generalization gap is primarily driven by inter-laboratory measurement noise rather than model architecture or threshold choices. Novel-target prediction remains fundamentally limited by data quality issues. The authors release PROTAC-Bench and associated frameworks to facilitate further research.
